title: Home
text:
What this site is about
Delta Point is my personal blog that I created while working on a university project analysing the relationship between the tone of Federal Reserve communications and financial markets. I’m planning to use this blog to share my findings from various interesting topics of the sector, and hopefully spark some interesting discussions. If you have any questions or suggestions, feel free to reach out! Thanks for visiting Delta One Research.

title: Posts
text:
Delta One Research

Reading the Fed: A Text-Based Analysis of FOMC Statements and Market Reactions








Apr 12, 2026


David Papinashvili





No matching items

title: Reading the Fed: A Text-Based Analysis of FOMC Statements and Market Reactions
text:
Introduction
Financial markets react not only to interest rate decisions, but also to the language used by central banks. The Federal Open Market Committee releases statements after policy meetings, and these statements can shape expectations about inflation, growth, and future interest rates.
This project investigates whether the tone of FOMC statements is associated with short-run movements in financial markets. I use Python to scrape FOMC statement text, construct a dictionary-based hawkish/dovish tone score, merge this with daily market data, and examine the relationship between statement tone and three market variables: the US 2-year Treasury yield, the S&P 500, and the US dollar index.
The aim is not to build a trading model. Instead, the project tests whether a simple text-based measure of central bank communication contains information that appears in market reactions.

title: Reading the Fed: A Text-Based Analysis of FOMC Statements and Market Reactions
text:
Data
The project combines two types of data. The first dataset contains FOMC statement text scraped from the Federal Reserve website. Each statement is treated as a policy communication event. The second dataset contains daily financial market data, including the US 2-year Treasury yield, the S&P 500, and the US dollar index.
After cleaning the statement text and preparing the market data, I merged both datasets by date. The final analysis dataset contains one row per FOMC statement and links the tone of that statement to same-date daily market movements.



Variable
Definition




tone_score
Hawkish term count minus dovish term count


tone_score_norm
Tone score divided by statement word count


us2yield_change
Daily change in the US 2-year Treasury yield


sp500_return
Daily percentage return of the S&P 500


usd_index_change
Daily percentage change in the US dollar index

title: Reading the Fed: A Text-Based Analysis of FOMC Statements and Market Reactions
text:
Methodology
The text analysis uses a dictionary-based approach. I created lists of hawkish and dovish monetary-policy terms. Hawkish terms include words and phrases related to inflation pressure, policy tightening, and economic strength. Dovish terms include words and phrases related to slowing growth, easier policy, uncertainty, and financial stress.
The raw tone score is calculated as:
\[
\text{Tone Score} = \text{Hawkish Count} - \text{Dovish Count}\
\]
Because FOMC statements vary in length, I also calculated a normalised score:
\[
\text{Normalised Tone Score} = \frac{\text{Tone Score}}{\text{Word Count}}
\]
This makes the tone measure more comparable across statements.
As a robustness check, I compared a baseline tone dictionary with an expanded dictionary. The correlation between the two normalised tone scores was approximately 0.96, suggesting that the main tone pattern is not highly sensitive to the exact dictionary choice.
Rows loaded: 43

title: Reading the Fed: A Text-Based Analysis of FOMC Statements and Market Reactions
text:
Results: tone over time
The time-series plot shows that the tone score varies across FOMC meetings. Some statements contain a higher concentration of hawkish-coded language, especially where inflation pressure, policy firming, or strong labour market conditions are discussed.
However, the tone score should not be interpreted as a complete measure of the Federal Reserve’s policy stance. For example, a statement may mention inflation frequently while still describing an accommodative policy environment. The score measures language frequency, not the full policy position.

title: Reading the Fed: A Text-Based Analysis of FOMC Statements and Market Reactions
text:
Results: market reactions
The clearest relationship appears between the normalised tone score and changes in the US 2-year Treasury yield. This is economically intuitive because the 2-year yield is sensitive to expectations about future monetary policy.
A more hawkish statement may lead investors to expect higher future interest rates, which can push short-term yields upward. The relationship is not perfect, but it is stronger than the relationships observed for the S&P 500 and the US dollar index.

S&P 500
The relationship between FOMC tone and S&P 500 returns is weaker. This is not surprising because equities respond to several channels at once. Hawkish language may be negative for equities through higher discount rates, but it may also reflect a stronger economy, which can support earnings expectations.




USD
The relationship with the US dollar index is also weaker. Exchange rates are affected by US monetary policy expectations, but also by foreign interest rates, global risk sentiment, and international capital flows. A simple FOMC tone score is therefore unlikely to explain most dollar movements.

title: Reading the Fed: A Text-Based Analysis of FOMC Statements and Market Reactions
text:
Limitations
There are several limitations to this analysis.
First, the tone score is dictionary-based. It counts words and phrases but does not fully understand context. For example, the word “inflation” may appear in both hawkish and dovish contexts depending on whether the statement says inflation is rising, falling, or expected to moderate.
Second, the sample size is relatively small because FOMC statements are event-based rather than daily observations.
Third, the market reaction variables use daily data. This means the results may include other information released on the same day, including the policy rate decision, press conference, projections, or broader market news. Therefore, the regressions should be interpreted as associations rather than causal estimates.
Fourth, the project does not control for other macroeconomic variables, monetary policy surprises, or market expectations before the announcement.

title: Reading the Fed: A Text-Based Analysis of FOMC Statements and Market Reactions
text:
Conclusion
This project finds that FOMC statement tone appears most closely related to changes in the US 2-year Treasury yield. This result is consistent with the idea that short-term yields are especially sensitive to central bank communication and expectations about future monetary policy.
The evidence is weaker for the S&P 500 and the US dollar index. This likely reflects the fact that equities and exchange rates are influenced by a broader set of forces than FOMC statement language alone.
Overall, the project shows that even a simple text-based approach can extract useful information from central bank communication. At the same time, the results should be interpreted cautiously because the tone measure is simple and market reactions are affected by many factors beyond statement language.
